original_abstract:
In this paper, we examine the challenges of selecting and assessing classification and regression models using cross-validation. Current state-of-the-art techniques often yield models with high variance, making them unreliable for sensitive practical applications such as Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) modeling. We identify and evaluate best practices designed to improve model reliability and increase confidence in the selection process. A central component of our approach is the integration of cloud computing, which facilitates the routine implementation of computationally intensive methods that were previously considered kitchen-sink or infeasible.

Specifically, we detail algorithms for repeated grid-search V-fold cross-validation for parameter tuning and define a repeated nested cross-validation framework for rigorous model assessment. We also address the critical issue of performing variable selection within the cross-validation loop to avoid selection bias. Testing these methods on seven QSAR datasets, we demonstrate that the significant variation in prediction performance—driven simply by how a dataset is split—must be accounted for when determining model quality. Our results emphasize the necessity of repeating both standard cross-validation for model selection and nested cross-validation for error estimation to ensure robust and reproducible scientific outcomes.
humanized_abstract:
The choice and evaluation of classification and regression models using cross-validation is a pressing problem that has not yet been fully addressed in the state-of-the-art. Models generated by the commonly used state-of-the-art methods are frequently of high variance and hence not reliable enough for the sensitive application domain of QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship). Best practices to achieve higher reliability and thereby higher confidence in the choice of models are sought. With the widespread adoption of cloud computing, expensive computer resources become available in an unprecedented fashion in our daily kitchens and thus can be used in a routine manner for resource-intensive modeling strategies that are traditionally regarded as kitchen-sink or even infeasible.

We present in detail the algorithms used for the repeated grid-search V-fold cross-validation for parameter tuning, as well as a repeated nested cross-validation scheme for model evaluation. In addition, we address the problem of variable selection within the cross-validation loop to avoid any selection bias. The validity of the proposed approach is demonstrated on seven QSAR collections of data where the high number of variation of prediction performances obtained are shown to be mainly due to splitting the data into training and test sets rather than to the choice of the model or the variables. As a consequence, to assess adequately the reliability of the model performances and of the variable selection, the procedure of standard cross-validation for the model selection and that of nested cross-validation for the evaluation of the errors should be repeated several times.

document.input:
In this paper, we examine the challenges of selecting and assessing classification and regression models using cross-validation. Current state-of-the-art techniques often yield models with high variance, making them unreliable for sensitive practical applications such as Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) modeling.
